The other options — 'Factor the equation', 'Use the quadratic formula', and 'Divide everything by F' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which equation represents a circle with center (−1, 4) and radius 3?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Equations of Circles ⭐ Standard Form The equation of a circle with center (h, k) and radius r is: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r² General Form x² + y² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Convert to standard form by completing the square. Key Skills Write the equation given center and radius.

quiz_questions.question_text: The general form of a circle equation is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Equations of Circles ⭐ Standard Form The equation of a circle with center (h, k) and radius r is: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r² General Form x² + y² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Convert to standard form by completing the square. Write the equation given center and a point on the circle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convert x² + y² − 6x + 4y − 12 = 0 to standard form. What is the radius?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Equations of Circles ⭐ Standard Form The equation of a circle with center (h, k) and radius r is: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r² General Form x² + y² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Convert to standard form by completing the square. The other options — '25', '12', and '√12' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Conic Sections (Intro) ⭐

What Are Conic Sections?

Curves formed by the intersection of a plane and a double cone: circle, ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola.

Overview

  • Circle: plane is perpendicular to the axis of the cone.
  • Ellipse: plane intersects at an angle, cutting through one nappe.
  • Parabola: plane is parallel to one slant side of the cone.
  • Hyperbola: plane intersects both nappes of the cone.

General Second-Degree Equation

Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0

Identify the conic by comparing A and C: if A = C → circle; if A ≠ C but same sign → ellipse; if A or C = 0 → parabola; if A and C have opposite signs → hyperbola.

quiz_questions.question_text: Conic sections are formed by the intersection of a plane and a:
quiz_questions.explanation: The intersection point is where two equations have the same x and y values. Set the expressions equal and solve. The intersection is Double cone. The other options — 'Sphere', 'Cylinder', and 'Prism' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the general equation Ax² + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0, if A = C, the conic is a:
quiz_questions.explanation: General Second-Degree Equation Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Identify the conic by comparing A and C: if A = C → circle; if A ≠ C but same sign → ellipse; if A or C = 0 → parabola; if A and C have opposite signs → hyperbola. Overview Circle — plane is perpendicular to the axis of the cone.

quiz_questions.question_text: A parabola is defined as the set of all points equidistant from:
quiz_questions.explanation: A parabola is the set of all points equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix). The vertex is the midpoint between them. The answer is 'A focus and a directrix'. The other options — 'Two foci', 'The center and a vertex', and 'Two directrices' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For an ellipse, the sum of the distances from any point on the ellipse to the two foci is:
quiz_questions.explanation: An ellipse is the set of all points where the sum of distances from two foci is constant. The standard form is x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 (a > b). The answer is Constant. The other options — 'Variable', 'Equal to zero', and 'Equal to the eccentricity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The eccentricity of a circle is:
quiz_questions.explanation: General Second-Degree Equation Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Identify the conic by comparing A and C: if A = C → circle; if A ≠ C but same sign → ellipse; if A or C = 0 → parabola; if A and C have opposite signs → hyperbola. The other options — '1', 'Greater than 1', and 'Between 0 and 1 (exclusive)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the equation Ax² + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0, if A and C have opposite signs, the conic is a:
quiz_questions.explanation: General Second-Degree Equation Ax² + Bxy + Cy² + Dx + Ey + F = 0 Identify the conic by comparing A and C: if A = C → circle; if A ≠ C but same sign → ellipse; if A or C = 0 → parabola; if A and C have opposite signs → hyperbola. Curves formed by the intersection of a plane and a double cone: circle , ellipse , parabola , and hyperbola.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hyperbola is defined by the property that the __________ of distances from any point to the two foci is constant.
quiz_questions.explanation: A hyperbola is the set of all points where the absolute difference of distances from two foci is constant. Standard form: x²/a² - y²/b² = 1. The answer is Difference. The other options — 'Sum', 'Product', and 'Ratio' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Areas of Parallelograms and Triangles
summary_sections.content_html:
Area of a Parallelogram

A = bh

where b is the base and h is the height (perpendicular to the base).

Area of a Triangle

A = ½bh

where b is the base and h is the corresponding height.

Key Ideas

  • The height must be perpendicular to the base — it is not necessarily a side of the figure.
  • Any side can be chosen as the base; the height changes accordingly.
  • A diagonal divides a parallelogram into two congruent triangles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a parallelogram with a base of 10 cm and a height of 6 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Parallelogram A = bh where b is the base and h is the height (perpendicular to the base). Area of a Triangle A = ½bh where b is the base and h is the corresponding height. The other options — '30 cm²', '80 cm²', and '16 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a triangle with a base of 14 cm and a height of 9 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is A = ½ × base × height. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — '126 cm²', '56 cm²', and '72 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The height used to calculate the area of a parallelogram must be:
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Parallelogram A = bh where b is the base and h is the height (perpendicular to the base). Key Ideas The height must be perpendicular to the base — it is not necessarily a side of the figure. The other options — 'Equal to the base', 'The longest side', and 'Parallel to the base' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into:
quiz_questions.explanation: A diagonal divides a parallelogram into two congruent triangles. The other options — 'Two similar triangles', 'Two right triangles', and 'Two equal rectangles' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Find the area of a triangle with sides a = 8 and b = 12 and included angle C = 30°.
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is A = ½ × base × height. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — '48 units²', '96 units²', and '36 units²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which formula correctly gives the area of a triangle using two sides and the included angle?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is A = ½ × base × height. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — 'A = ab sin C', 'A = ab cos C', and 'A = ½ab cos C' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A parallelogram has a base of 15 m and an area of 120 m². What is its height?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Parallelogram A = bh where b is the base and h is the height (perpendicular to the base). Area of a Triangle A = ½bh where b is the base and h is the corresponding height. The other options — '10 m', '6 m', and '12 m' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

u11_l11.2.title: Areas of Trapezoids, Rhombi, and Kites

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Areas of Trapezoids, Rhombi, and Kites
summary_sections.content_html:
Area of a Trapezoid

A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h

where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height.

Area of a Rhombus or Kite

A = ½d₁d₂

where d₁ and d₂ are the lengths of the diagonals.

Key Ideas

  • The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors of each other.
  • The diagonals of a kite are perpendicular, but only one is bisected.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a trapezoid with bases 8 cm and 14 cm and a height of 5 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — '110 cm²', '70 cm²', and '44 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A rhombus has diagonals of length 10 cm and 16 cm. What is its area?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Rhombus or Kite A = ½d₁d₂ where d₁ and d₂ are the lengths of the diagonals. Key Ideas The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors of each other. The other options — '160 cm²', '40 cm²', and '120 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a kite with diagonals of 12 m and 7 m?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Rhombus or Kite A = ½d₁d₂ where d₁ and d₂ are the lengths of the diagonals. The diagonals of a kite are perpendicular, but only one is bisected. The other options — '84 m²', '38 m²', and '19 m²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The median of a trapezoid with bases 6 and 18 is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — '6', '18', and '24' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which property do both rhombi and kites share?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Ideas The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors of each other. The diagonals of a kite are perpendicular, but only one is bisected. The other options — 'All sides are equal', 'Diagonals bisect each other', and 'Four right angles' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A trapezoid has a median of 10 cm and a height of 8 cm. What is its area?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — '40 cm²', '160 cm²', and '100 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which formula is used to find the area of a trapezoid?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — 'A = b₁ × b₂ × h', 'A = (b₁ + b₂) × h', and 'A = ½ × b₁ × b₂' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Areas of Circles and Sectors
summary_sections.content_html:
Area of a Circle

A = πr²

Area of a Sector

A sector is a "pie slice" of a circle defined by a central angle θ.

A = (θ/360°) × πr²

Area of a Segment

A segment is the region between a chord and its arc.

A(segment) = A(sector) − A(triangle)

Key Ideas

  • The ratio of a sector's area to the circle's area equals the ratio of the central angle to 360°.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a circle with radius 5 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a circle is A = πr². Substitute the radius and calculate. The other options — '10π cm²', '50π cm²', and '5π cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a sector with radius 10 cm and central angle 90°?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Circle A = πr² Area of a Sector A sector is a "pie slice" of a circle defined by a central angle θ. A(segment) = A(sector) − A(triangle) Key Ideas The ratio of a sector's area to the circle's area equals the ratio of the central angle to 360°.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the arc length of a sector with radius 6 cm and central angle 60°?
quiz_questions.explanation: When two chords intersect inside a circle, the angle = ½(sum of intercepted arcs). Also, the products of their segments are equal: (segment₁)(segment₂) = (segment₃)(segment₄). The answer is '2Ï€ cm'. The other options — '6Ï€ cm', '12Ï€ cm', and '3Ï€ cm' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The area of a segment of a circle is found by:

options.text: Adding the sector area and the triangle area
Subtracting the triangle area from the sector area
Dividing the circle area by the angle

quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a circle is A = πr². Substitute the radius and calculate. The other options — 'Adding the sector area and the triangle area', 'Multiplying the sector area by 2', and 'Dividing the circle area by the angle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a semicircle with diameter 12?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a circle is A = πr². Substitute the radius and calculate. The other options — '36Ï€', '72Ï€', and '12Ï€' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which formula gives the area of a sector with central angle θ and radius r?
quiz_questions.explanation: A = (θ/360°) × πr² Area of a Segment A segment is the region between a chord and its arc. Area of a Circle A = πr² Area of a Sector A sector is a "pie slice" of a circle defined by a central angle θ. The other options — 'A = (θ/180°) × πr²', 'A = (θ/360°) × 2πr', and 'A = θ × πr²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A circle has an area of 64π cm². What is its radius?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a circle is A = πr². Substitute the radius and calculate. The other options — '32 cm', '16 cm', and '4 cm' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

u11_l11.4.title: Areas of Regular Polygons and Composite Figures

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Areas of Regular Polygons and Composite Figures
summary_sections.content_html:
Area of a Regular Polygon

A = ½aP

where a is the apothem (distance from center to a side) and P is the perimeter.

Key Definitions

  • Center: equidistant from all vertices.
  • Radius: segment from center to a vertex.
  • Apothem: perpendicular segment from center to a side.
  • Central angle of a regular n-gon = 360°/n.

Composite Figures

Break a composite figure into non-overlapping simple shapes. Find each area and add (or subtract for holes).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area of a regular polygon with an apothem of 4 cm and a perimeter of 24 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Regular Polygon A = ½aP where a is the apothem (distance from center to a side) and P is the perimeter. The other options — '96 cm²', '28 cm²', and '64 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the central angle of a regular hexagon?
quiz_questions.explanation: A regular hexagon has 6 equal sides and interior angles of 120° each. Sum of interior angles = (n-2)×180° = 720°. The answer is 60°. The other options — '45°', '72°', and '90°' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The apothem of a regular polygon is defined as:

options.text: The distance from the center to a vertex
The perpendicular distance from the center to the midpoint of a side

quiz_questions.explanation: Area of a Regular Polygon A = ½aP where a is the apothem (distance from center to a side) and P is the perimeter. Apothem — perpendicular segment from center to a side. The other options — 'The distance from the center to a vertex', 'The length of a side', and 'The diagonal of the polygon' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the interior angle of a regular octagon?
quiz_questions.explanation: Central angle of a regular n-gon = 360°/n. The other options — '120°', '140°', and '150°' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: To find the area of a composite figure, you should:

options.text: Break it into simple shapes, then add or subtract their areas
Use only the largest shape's area
Average the areas of all shapes

quiz_questions.explanation: Composite Figures Break a composite figure into non-overlapping simple shapes. Find each area and add (or subtract for holes). The other options — 'Multiply all the dimensions together', 'Use only the largest shape's area', and 'Average the areas of all shapes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the central angle of a regular pentagon?
quiz_questions.explanation: Central angle of a regular n-gon = 360°/n. The other options — '60°', '108°', and '90°' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A regular polygon has an apothem of 5√3 cm and a perimeter of 60 cm. What is its area?
quiz_questions.explanation: Simplify radicals by factoring out perfect squares: √(n) = √(a²·b) = a√b. The result is 150√3 cm². The other options — '300 cm²', '600√3 cm²', and '300√3 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Areas of Similar Figures
summary_sections.content_html:
Area Ratio Theorem

If two similar figures have a scale factor of a : b, then the ratio of their areas is a² : b².

Example

If two similar triangles have sides in ratio 3 : 5, their areas are in ratio 9 : 25.

Key Skills

  • Find the ratio of areas given the scale factor.
  • Find the scale factor given the ratio of areas (take the square root).
  • Use area ratios to find unknown areas of similar figures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar figures have a scale factor of 3:5. What is the ratio of their areas?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is 9:25. The other options — '3:5', '6:10', and '27:125' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar triangles have areas of 16 cm² and 64 cm². What is the scale factor of their corresponding sides?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is A = ½ × base × height. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — '1:4', '2:4', and '4:16' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the scale factor between two similar figures is 2:7, what is the ratio of their perimeters?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is 2:7. The other options — '4:49', '4:14', and '8:343' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A figure has an area of 50 cm². A similar figure has a scale factor of 3 relative to the original. What is the area of the larger figure?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is 450 cm². The other options — '150 cm²', '250 cm²', and '500 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The ratio of the areas of two similar pentagons is 25:81. What is the ratio of their corresponding sides?
quiz_questions.explanation: Example If two similar triangles have sides in ratio 3 : 5, their areas are in ratio 9 : 25. Area Ratio Theorem If two similar figures have a scale factor of a : b , then the ratio of their areas is a² : b². The other options — '25:81', '625:6561', and '12.5:40.5' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the area ratio rule for similar figures apply only to triangles?

options.text: No, it works for all similar figures

quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a triangle is A = ½ × base × height. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — 'Yes, only triangles', 'Only triangles and rectangles', and 'Only regular polygons' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar rectangles have perimeters of 20 cm and 30 cm. If the smaller has an area of 24 cm², what is the area of the larger?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a rectangle is A = length × width. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — '36 cm²', '48 cm²', and '72 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Integration in Area Calculations ⭐

Area Under a Curve

The area between a curve y = f(x) and the x-axis from x = a to x = b can be approximated using geometric methods.

Riemann Sums

  • Divide the interval [a, b] into n equal subintervals of width Δx = (b − a)/n.
  • Approximate area = sum of rectangle areas = Σ f(xᵢ) × Δx.
  • As n → ∞, the approximation approaches the exact area.

Connection to Geometry

Area formulas for trapezoids and triangles are special cases of integration for linear functions. Circles and sectors relate to integration of curves.

quiz_questions.question_text: What method approximates the area under a curve by dividing it into rectangles?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a rectangle is A = length × width. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — 'Cavalieri's Principle', 'Trapezoidal rule', and 'Simpson's rule' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When dividing the interval [a, b] into n subintervals, what is the width Δx of each subinterval?
quiz_questions.explanation: Interval notation: use [ ] for included endpoints (≤, ≥) and ( ) for excluded endpoints (<, >). Use ∞ or -∞ for unbounded intervals (always with parentheses). The answer is (b − a) / n. The other options — '(a + b) / n', 'n / (b − a)', and '(b − a) × n' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the area under y = x from x = 0 to x = 4?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a curve y = f(x) from a to b is found by integration: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. For simple shapes (triangles, trapezoids), use geometry formulas. The answer is 8. The other options — '4', '16', and '12' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: As the number of subintervals n approaches infinity, the Riemann sum approximation becomes:
quiz_questions.explanation: Interval notation: use [ ] for included endpoints (≤, ≥) and ( ) for excluded endpoints (<, >). Use ∞ or -∞ for unbounded intervals (always with parentheses). The answer is The exact area. The other options — 'Less accurate', 'Zero', and 'Infinity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which method uses trapezoids instead of rectangles for a more accurate area approximation?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a rectangle is A = length × width. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — 'Left Riemann sum', 'Right Riemann sum', and 'Midpoint rule' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cavalieri's Principle states that two figures have the same area if:

options.text: They have the same width at every height

quiz_questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: two figures with equal cross-sectional widths at every height have equal areas (2D) or volumes (3D). The answer is They have the same width at every height. The other options — 'They have the same perimeter', 'They are congruent', and 'They are similar figures' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The value of π can be derived geometrically by integrating:
quiz_questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Circular rings'. The other options — 'Rectangular strips', 'Triangular wedges', and 'Parabolic arcs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Representations of Three-Dimensional Figures
summary_sections.content_html:
Cross Sections

A cross section is the intersection of a solid and a plane. The shape depends on the solid and the angle of the plane.

Examples

  • A plane parallel to the base of a cylinder creates a circle.
  • A plane through the apex of a cone creates a triangle.
  • A plane at an angle through a cylinder creates an ellipse.

Nets

A net is a two-dimensional pattern that folds to form a three-dimensional figure. Nets are useful for visualizing and calculating surface area.

Orthographic and Isometric Views

  • Top view, front view, and side view are orthographic projections.
  • Isometric view shows three faces of a solid in one drawing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a cross section of a solid?

options.text: The shadow cast by a solid onto a flat surface
The intersection of a solid and a plane
A 2D pattern that folds into a 3D figure
The surface area of one face of the solid

quiz_questions.explanation: Cross Sections A cross section is the intersection of a solid and a plane. The shape depends on the solid and the angle of the plane. The other options — 'The shadow cast by a solid onto a flat surface', 'A 2D pattern that folds into a 3D figure', and 'The surface area of one face of the solid' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a net in geometry?

options.text: A wireframe model of a 3D figure
A top-down view of a 3D solid
A 2D pattern that can be folded into a 3D figure
A grid used to measure the volume of a solid

quiz_questions.explanation: Nets A net is a two-dimensional pattern that folds to form a three-dimensional figure. The other options — 'A wireframe model of a 3D figure', 'A top-down view of a 3D solid', and 'A grid used to measure the volume of a solid' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to Euler's formula, if a polyhedron has 8 vertices and 12 edges, how many faces does it have?
quiz_questions.explanation: Euler's formula for polyhedra: V - E + F = 2, where V = vertices, E = edges, F = faces. Substitute the known values and solve for the unknown. The answer is 6. The other options — '4', '8', and '10' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of drawing shows the top, front, and side views of a 3D object separately?
quiz_questions.explanation: Orthographic and Isometric Views Top view , front view , and side view are orthographic projections. The other options — 'Isometric drawing', 'Perspective drawing', and 'Cross-sectional drawing' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a possible cross section of a cube?
quiz_questions.explanation: Examples A plane parallel to the base of a cylinder creates a circle. Cross Sections A cross section is the intersection of a solid and a plane. The other options — 'Triangle', 'Hexagon', and 'Pentagon' — are all valid examples, making 'Circle' the correct exception.

quiz_questions.question_text: What distinguishes a prism from a pyramid?

options.text: A prism has one base; a pyramid has two bases
A prism has curved faces; a pyramid has flat faces

u12_l12.2.title: Surface Areas of Prisms and Cylinders

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Surface Areas of Prisms and Cylinders
summary_sections.content_html:
Lateral Area of a Prism

L = Ph

where P is the perimeter of the base and h is the height.

Surface Area of a Prism

S = Ph + 2B

where B is the area of one base.

Lateral Area of a Cylinder

L = 2πrh

Surface Area of a Cylinder

S = 2πrh + 2πr²

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Volumes of Prisms and Cylinders
summary_sections.content_html:
Volume of a Prism

V = Bh

where B is the area of the base and h is the height.

Volume of a Cylinder

V = πr²h

Oblique Prisms and Cylinders

Cavalieri's Principle: If two solids have the same height and the same cross-sectional area at every level, they have the same volume. So oblique prisms/cylinders use the same formulas.

The answer is If two solids have the same height and the same cross-sectional area at every level, they have the same volume. The other options — 'Two solids with the same height always have the same volume', 'Two solids with the same surface area have the same volume', and 'Oblique solids always have less volume than right solids' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Volumes of Pyramids and Cones
summary_sections.content_html:
Volume of a Pyramid

V = ⅓Bh

where B is the area of the base and h is the height.

Volume of a Cone

V = ⅓πr²h

Key Relationship

The volume of a pyramid (or cone) is exactly one-third the volume of a prism (or cylinder) with the same base and height.

u12_l12.6.title: Surface Areas and Volumes of Spheres

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Surface Areas and Volumes of Spheres
summary_sections.content_html:
Surface Area of a Sphere

S = 4πr²

Volume of a Sphere

V = (4/3)πr³

Hemisphere

  • Surface area (total) = 3πr² (curved surface + base circle).
  • Volume = (2/3)πr³.

Great Circle

A great circle is the intersection of a sphere and a plane through the center. Its circumference equals the circumference of the sphere.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Spherical Geometry ⭐

Lines in Spherical Geometry

In spherical geometry, "lines" are great circles. There are no parallel lines — every pair of great circles intersects at two points.

Triangles on a Sphere

  • The sum of angles in a spherical triangle is greater than 180° and less than 540°.
  • The excess over 180° is related to the triangle's area.

Comparing Euclidean and Spherical Geometry

  • Euclidean: through a point not on a line, exactly one parallel exists (Parallel Postulate).
  • Spherical: no parallels exist — all great circles intersect.
  • Euclidean: triangle angle sum = 180°. Spherical: triangle angle sum > 180°.

quiz_questions.question_text: In spherical geometry, what takes the place of straight lines?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Spherical Geometry ⭐ Lines in Spherical Geometry In spherical geometry, "lines" are great circles. There are no parallel lines — every pair of great circles intersects at two points. The other options — 'Small circles', 'Arcs of latitude', and 'Tangent lines' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

The answer is The shortest path between two points, which is a great circle arc. The other options — 'Any path on the surface of the sphere', 'A line of latitude', and 'A spiral path connecting the poles' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Congruent and Similar Solids
summary_sections.content_html:
Congruent Solids

Two solids are congruent if they have exactly the same shape and size — all corresponding measurements are equal.

Similar Solids

Two solids are similar if they have the same shape and their corresponding measurements are proportional with scale factor k.

Ratios for Similar Solids (scale factor a : b)

  • Corresponding lengths: a : b
  • Surface areas: a² : b²
  • Volumes: a³ : b³

Example

If two similar cylinders have radii in ratio 2 : 5, their volumes are in ratio 8 : 125.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two solids are similar with a scale factor of k. What is the ratio of their surface areas?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is k². The other options — 'k', 'k³', and '2k' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar solids have a scale factor of k. What is the ratio of their volumes?
quiz_questions.explanation: Factor the expression by finding common factors or using patterns (difference of squares, trinomial factoring, etc.). The factored form is k³. The other options — 'k', 'k²', and '2k³' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar cylinders have surface areas of 25Ï€ and 100Ï€. What is the scale factor?
quiz_questions.explanation: The surface area of a cylinder is SA = 2πr² + 2πrh (two circles plus the lateral surface). The other options — '1:4', '1:3', and '5:10' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: Same surface area but different volumes

Similar Solids Two solids are similar if they have the same shape and their corresponding measurements are proportional with scale factor k.

quiz_questions.explanation: For similar figures with scale factor k: lengths scale by k, areas by k², and volumes by k³. If the scale is 1/n, the volume ratio is 1/n³. The answer is 1/13,824. The other options — '1/24', '1/576', and '1/48' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two similar prisms have volumes of 64 cm³ and 512 cm³. What is the scale factor?
quiz_questions.explanation: The volume of a rectangular prism is V = length × width × height. The other options — '1:4', '1:3', and '1:8' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

options.text: Take the cube root of the surface area ratio
Take the square root of the surface area ratio

The factored form is Take the square root of the surface area ratio. The other options — 'Divide the surface areas', 'Take the cube root of the surface area ratio', and 'Multiply the surface areas' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Cavalieri's Principle and Applications ⭐

Cavalieri's Principle

If two solids have the same height and every cross section at the same height has the same area, then the two solids have the same volume.

Applications

  • Proves that oblique prisms and right prisms with the same base and height have equal volumes.
  • Proves the volume formula for a sphere by comparing cross sections with a cylinder-minus-cone figure.

Deriving the Sphere Volume

At height h from the center, the cross section of a sphere of radius r is a circle with area π(r² − h²). This matches a cylinder of radius r with a cone of radius r removed — hence V(sphere) = V(cylinder) − V(cone) = πr²(2r) − ⅓πr²(2r) = (4/3)πr³.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does Cavalieri's Principle state?

options.text: Two solids with equal heights always have equal volumes
Two solids with equal surface areas have equal volumes
If two solids have the same height and equal cross-sectional areas at every level, they have the same volume
Two solids with equal bases always have equal volumes

quiz_questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: two figures with equal cross-sectional widths at every height have equal areas (2D) or volumes (3D). The answer is If two solids have the same height and equal cross-sectional areas at every level, they have the same volume. The other options — 'Two solids with equal heights always have equal volumes', 'Two solids with equal surface areas have equal volumes', and 'Two solids with equal bases always have equal volumes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which everyday analogy best illustrates Cavalieri's Principle?

options.text: A stack of coins — shifting them sideways doesn't change total volume
Cutting a cake into equal slices

quiz_questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: two figures with equal cross-sectional widths at every height have equal areas (2D) or volumes (3D). The answer is A stack of coins — shifting them sideways doesn't change total volume. The other options — 'Pouring water between different containers', 'Cutting a cake into equal slices', and 'Stacking blocks of different sizes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cavalieri's Principle proves that an oblique cylinder and a right cylinder with the same base and height have:
quiz_questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: two figures with equal cross-sectional widths at every height have equal areas (2D) or volumes (3D). The answer is The same volume. The other options — 'Different volumes', 'The same surface area', and 'The same lateral area' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the method of disks, what shape are the thin cross-sections used?
quiz_questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Circles (disks)'. The other options — 'Rectangles', 'Triangles', and 'Squares' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is the volume of a sphere derived using Cavalieri's Principle?

options.text: By filling a sphere with water and measuring the displacement
By comparing cross-sections of a hemisphere with a cylinder minus a cone
By stacking spheres inside a cube
By unrolling the sphere into a flat rectangle

quiz_questions.explanation: The volume of a sphere is V = (4/3)πr³. Substitute the radius and calculate. The other options — 'By filling a sphere with water and measuring the displacement', 'By stacking spheres inside a cube', and 'By unrolling the sphere into a flat rectangle' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can Cavalieri's Principle be applied in 2D?

options.text: No, it only applies to 3D solids
Yes — if two regions have the same height and equal widths at every level, they have the same area
Only when both shapes are congruent

quiz_questions.explanation: Cavalieri's Principle: two figures with equal cross-sectional widths at every height have equal areas (2D) or volumes (3D). The answer is Yes — if two regions have the same height and equal widths at every level, they have the same area. The other options — 'No, it only applies to 3D solids', 'Only for circles and rectangles', and 'Only when both shapes are congruent' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two solids both have a height of 10 cm. At every height, both have a cross-sectional area of 15 cm². What can you conclude?

options.text: They have the same surface area
They have the same volume of 150 cm³
They must be the same shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Cavalieri's Principle and Applications ⭐ Cavalieri's Principle If two solids have the same height and every cross section at the same height has the same area, then the two solids have the same volume. Deriving the Sphere Volume At height h from the center, the cross section of a sphere of radius r is a circle with area π(r² − h²).

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Representing Sample Spaces
summary_sections.content_html:
Definitions

  • An experiment is a situation involving chance.
  • An outcome is a possible result of an experiment.
  • A sample space is the set of all possible outcomes.
  • An event is a subset of the sample space.

Methods of Representation

  • Organized list: write out all outcomes systematically.
  • Tree diagram: branch for each choice; total outcomes = product of branch counts.
  • Table: arrange outcomes in rows and columns for two-stage experiments.

Fundamental Counting Principle

If event A has m outcomes and event B has n outcomes, then A followed by B has m × n outcomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a sample space in probability?

options.text: The most likely outcome of an experiment
A single result from an experiment
The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment
A subset of outcomes that we are interested in

quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. The other options — 'The most likely outcome of an experiment', 'A single result from an experiment', and 'A subset of outcomes that we are interested in' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many outcomes are in the sample space when flipping 3 coins?
quiz_questions.explanation: A sample space is the set of all possible outcomes. An event is a subset of the sample space. The other options — '6', '3', and '12' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the Fundamental Counting Principle, how many outcomes are there when rolling two dice?
quiz_questions.explanation: Fundamental Counting Principle If event A has m outcomes and event B has n outcomes, then A followed by B has m × n outcomes. The other options — '12', '24', and '18' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an event in probability?

options.text: A subset of the sample space
A single trial of an experiment
The number of times an experiment is repeated

quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives A subset of the sample space. The other options — 'The entire sample space', 'A single trial of an experiment', and 'The number of times an experiment is repeated' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A restaurant offers 4 appetizers, 6 entrées, and 3 desserts. How many different meals (one of each) are possible?
quiz_questions.explanation: Count arrangements using: permutations (order matters) = n!/(n-r)!, or combinations (order doesn't matter) = n!/[r!(n-r)!]. The answer is 72. The other options — '13', '48', and '24' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which tool is best for displaying the sample space of a two-stage experiment with different options at each stage?
quiz_questions.explanation: Tree diagram — branch for each choice; total outcomes = product of branch counts. A sample space is the set of all possible outcomes. The other options — 'Bar graph', 'Pie chart', and 'Venn diagram' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sample space is called uniform when:

options.text: There is only one possible outcome
The outcomes are listed in order
The sample space has an even number of outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: A sample space is the set of all possible outcomes. An event is a subset of the sample space. The other options — 'There is only one possible outcome', 'The outcomes are listed in order', and 'The sample space has an even number of outcomes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Permutations and Combinations
summary_sections.content_html:
Permutation

An arrangement of objects where order matters.

P(n, r) = n! / (n − r)!

Combination

A selection of objects where order does not matter.

C(n, r) = n! / [r!(n − r)!]

When to Use Which

  • Use permutations for arrangements, rankings, sequences, passwords.
  • Use combinations for groups, committees, selections.

Special Cases

  • 0! = 1
  • P(n, n) = n!
  • C(n, 0) = C(n, n) = 1

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the value of 5! (5 factorial)?
quiz_questions.explanation: n! (n factorial) = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1. For example, 5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120. By definition, 0! = 1. The answer is 120. The other options — '25', '60', and '15' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the value of P(6, 2)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Permutations P(n,r) = n! / (n-r)! count arrangements where order matters. Substitute the given values and simplify. The answer is 30. The other options — '15', '36', and '12' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 10 people?
quiz_questions.explanation: Count arrangements using: permutations (order matters) = n!/(n-r)!, or combinations (order doesn't matter) = n!/[r!(n-r)!]. The answer is 120. The other options — '720', '210', and '30' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the value of 0! ?
quiz_questions.explanation: n! (n factorial) = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1. For example, 5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120. By definition, 0! = 1. The answer is 1. The other options — '0', 'Undefined', and '−1' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When does order matter in counting?
quiz_questions.explanation: Combination A selection of objects where order does not matter. The other options — 'In combinations', 'In both permutations and combinations', and 'In neither permutations nor combinations' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In how many ways can 5 books be arranged on a shelf?
quiz_questions.explanation: Count arrangements using: permutations (order matters) = n!/(n-r)!, or combinations (order doesn't matter) = n!/[r!(n-r)!]. The answer is 120. The other options — '25', '10', and '60' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the value of C(8, 0)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Combinations C(n,r) = n! / [r!(n-r)!] count selections where order doesn't matter. Substitute the given values and simplify. The answer is 1. The other options — '8', '0', and '56' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Geometric Probability
summary_sections.content_html:
Definition

Geometric probability uses lengths, areas, or volumes to calculate the likelihood of an event.

Length Model

P(event) = favorable length / total length

Area Model

P(event) = favorable area / total area

Examples

  • Probability of a randomly thrown dart landing in a circle inscribed in a square: P = πr² / (2r)² = π/4 ≈ 0.785.
  • Probability of landing on a segment within a number line.

quiz_questions.question_text: In geometric probability, the probability of hitting a region is based on:

options.text: The number of points in the region
The ratio of the favorable area to the total area

quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives The ratio of the favorable area to the total area. The other options — 'The number of points in the region', 'The color of the region', and 'The perimeter of the region' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A dart is thrown randomly at a 10×10 square board. A circle of radius 3 is drawn at the center. What is the approximate probability of hitting the circle?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 0.283. The other options — '0.314', '0.30', and '0.09' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spinner is divided into sectors. A winning sector has a central angle of 90°. What is the probability of landing on the winning sector?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 1/4. The other options — '1/3', '1/2', and '1/6' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Three concentric circles have radii 1, 2, and 3. What is the probability that a randomly thrown dart lands in the innermost circle?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 1/9. The other options — '1/3', '1/6', and '1/4' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A segment is 20 cm long. A point is chosen randomly. What is the probability that the point is within the first 5 cm?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 1/4. The other options — '1/3', '1/5', and '1/2' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which geometric measure is used in the length model of geometric probability?

options.text: Favorable length divided by total length
Number of favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives Favorable length divided by total length. The other options — 'Volume', 'Number of favorable outcomes divided by total outcomes', and 'Circumference divided by diameter' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A circular dartboard has radius 10 in. A bullseye circle at the center has radius 2 in. What is the probability of hitting the bullseye?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 1/25. The other options — '1/10', '1/5', and '1/50' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Simulations
summary_sections.content_html:
Definition

A simulation is an experiment that models a real-world situation using random devices (coins, dice, random number generators).

Steps for a Simulation

  • 1. Define the problem and what outcome you want to estimate.
  • 2. Choose a model that has the same probabilities as the real situation.
  • 3. Run many trials of the simulation.
  • 4. Collect and analyze the results.
  • 5. Draw conclusions about the real situation.

Key Ideas

  • More trials generally give more reliable results (Law of Large Numbers).
  • Simulations are useful when theoretical probability is hard to calculate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a simulation in probability?

options.text: A mathematical proof of a theorem
A model that uses random devices to represent real-world situations
A diagram showing all possible outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives A model that uses random devices to represent real-world situations. The other options — 'A mathematical proof of a theorem', 'An exact calculation of probability', and 'A diagram showing all possible outcomes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: According to the Law of Large Numbers, what happens as the number of trials increases?

options.text: The experimental probability stays the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Simulations are useful when theoretical probability is hard to calculate. Key Ideas More trials generally give more reliable results ( Law of Large Numbers ). The other options — 'The probability becomes less predictable', 'The experimental probability stays the same', and 'The outcomes become more random' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the first step in designing a simulation?
quiz_questions.explanation: Define the problem and what outcome you want to estimate. 2. The other options — 'Run the trials', 'Analyze the results', and 'Choose a random number generator' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a "trial" mean in the context of a simulation?

options.text: The final result of the simulation
One complete run of the simulation
A test to see if the simulation works

quiz_questions.explanation: Run many trials of the simulation. 4. The other options — 'The final result of the simulation', 'The theoretical probability calculation', and 'A test to see if the simulation works' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: To simulate flipping a fair coin, which random number assignment would be appropriate?
quiz_questions.explanation: Expected value = probability × number of trials. For a fair die, each face has P = 1/6. Over n trials, expect about n/6 occurrences of each number. The answer is 1–5 = heads, 6–10 = tails (using digits 1–10). The other options — '1–3 = heads, 4 = tails', '1–7 = heads, 8–10 = tails', and '1 = heads, 2–6 = tails' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are more trials better in a simulation?

options.text: They make the simulation easier to set up
They guarantee the exact theoretical probability
They produce more reliable and accurate results
They reduce the number of possible outcomes

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Ideas More trials generally give more reliable results ( Law of Large Numbers ). The other options — 'They make the simulation easier to set up', 'They guarantee the exact theoretical probability', and 'They reduce the number of possible outcomes' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: You simulate rolling a die 600 times. About how many times would you expect to roll a 4?
quiz_questions.explanation: Expected value = probability × number of trials. For a fair die, each face has P = 1/6. Over n trials, expect about n/6 occurrences of each number. The answer is 100. The other options — '60', '150', and '200' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

u13_l13.5.title: Probabilities of Independent and Dependent Events

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Probabilities of Independent and Dependent Events
summary_sections.content_html:
Independent Events

Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the other.

P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)

Dependent Events

Two events are dependent if the outcome of one affects the other.

P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B | A)

Conditional Probability

P(B | A) is the probability of B occurring given that A has occurred.

Examples

  • Drawing cards with replacement → independent.
  • Drawing cards without replacement → dependent.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two events are independent if:

options.text: They cannot happen at the same time
The occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other
They share the same sample space

quiz_questions.explanation: Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). The answer is 'The occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other'. The other options — 'They cannot happen at the same time', 'They always happen together', and 'They share the same sample space' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. You draw one marble, replace it, then draw again. What is P(red, then blue)?
quiz_questions.explanation: With replacement: P(A then B) = P(A) × P(B) since events are independent. Without replacement: probabilities change after each draw. Multiply the individual probabilities. The answer is 15/64. The other options — '15/56', '8/64', and '3/8' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Drawing cards without replacement is an example of:
quiz_questions.explanation: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) Dependent Events Two events are dependent if the outcome of one affects the other. Independent Events Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the other. The other options — 'Independent events', 'Mutually exclusive events', and 'Complementary events' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For dependent events A and B, P(A and B) equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) Dependent Events Two events are dependent if the outcome of one affects the other. The other options — 'P(A) + P(B)', 'P(A) × P(B)', and 'P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bag has 4 green and 6 yellow marbles. You draw two marbles without replacement. What is P(green, then green)?
quiz_questions.explanation: With replacement: P(A then B) = P(A) × P(B) since events are independent. Without replacement: probabilities change after each draw. Multiply the individual probabilities. The answer is 12/90 (or 2/15). The other options — '16/100', '4/25', and '6/90' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How can you test whether events A and B are independent?

options.text: Check if P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)

quiz_questions.explanation: Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). The answer is 'Check if P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B)'. The other options — 'Check if P(A or B) = 0', 'Check if P(A) = P(B)', and 'Check if P(A) + P(B) = 1' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: P(B|A) represents:

options.text: The probability that A happens before B
The probability of B given that A has already occurred
The probability of A and B happening together
The probability that neither A nor B occurs

quiz_questions.explanation: The absolute value |x| gives the distance from zero, always non-negative. For equations |expression| = k, split into two cases: expression = k and expression = -k. The answer is The probability of B given that A has already occurred. The other options — 'The probability that A happens before B', 'The probability of A and B happening together', and 'The probability that neither A nor B occurs' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Probabilities of Mutually Exclusive Events
summary_sections.content_html:
Mutually Exclusive Events

Two events are mutually exclusive (disjoint) if they cannot happen at the same time.

P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)

Non-Mutually Exclusive Events

If events can happen at the same time:

P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)

Complement Rule

P(not A) = 1 − P(A)

Key Insight

Always check whether events overlap. If they do, subtract the overlap to avoid double counting.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two events are mutually exclusive if:

options.text: They cannot happen at the same time

quiz_questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot occur simultaneously: P(A and B) = 0. For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The answer is 'They cannot happen at the same time'. The other options — 'They always occur together', 'One event causes the other', and 'They have the same probability' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the probability of rolling a 2 or a 5 on a standard die?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 1/3. The other options — '1/6', '1/36', and '2/3' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For events that are NOT mutually exclusive, P(A or B) equals:
quiz_questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot occur simultaneously: P(A and B) = 0. For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The answer is 'P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)'. The other options — 'P(A) + P(B)', 'P(A) × P(B)', and 'P(A) − P(B)' — are all valid examples, making 'P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B)' the correct exception.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the probability of drawing a King or a Heart from a standard 52-card deck?
quiz_questions.explanation: Probability = (favorable outcomes) / (total outcomes). Working through the calculation gives 4/13. The other options — '17/52', '1/4', and '5/13' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If P(A) = 0.6, what is P(not A)?
quiz_questions.explanation: P(E) = 0 means the event is impossible; P(E) = 1 means it's certain. P(not E) = 1 - P(E) (complement rule). Probabilities always range from 0 to 1. The answer is 0.4. The other options — '0.6', '0.36', and '1.6' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which pair of events is mutually exclusive?

options.text: Drawing a red card and drawing a King
Rolling an even number and rolling a 6
Rolling a 3 and rolling a 5 on a single die
Being a student and being an athlete

quiz_questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot occur simultaneously: P(A and B) = 0. For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The answer is 'Rolling a 3 and rolling a 5 on a single die'. The other options — 'Drawing a red card and drawing a King', 'Rolling an even number and rolling a 6', and 'Being a student and being an athlete' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For mutually exclusive events A and B, what is P(A and B)?
quiz_questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot occur simultaneously: P(A and B) = 0. For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The answer is '0'. The other options — 'P(A) × P(B)', 'P(A) + P(B)', and '1' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Monte Carlo Methods in Geometry ⭐

What Is Monte Carlo?

A computational technique that uses random sampling to estimate geometric quantities like areas or volumes.

Estimating π

  • Inscribe a circle of radius r inside a square of side 2r.
  • Generate random points in the square.
  • Count how many fall inside the circle.
  • π ≈ 4 × (points inside circle) / (total points).

Estimating Irregular Areas

Enclose the region in a rectangle. Generate random points. The area ≈ (hits inside region / total points) × area of rectangle.

Accuracy

More random samples → better estimate. Error decreases proportionally to 1/√n, where n is the number of samples.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Monte Carlo method?

options.text: An exact algebraic method for computing area
A method that uses compasses and straightedges
A technique that uses random sampling to estimate geometric quantities
A card game played in casinos

quiz_questions.explanation: A computational technique that uses random sampling to estimate geometric quantities like areas or volumes. Key Concepts — Monte Carlo Methods in Geometry ⭐ What Is Monte Carlo? The other options — 'An exact algebraic method for computing area', 'A method that uses compasses and straightedges', and 'A card game played in casinos' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: To estimate π using Monte Carlo, you randomly generate points in a square and check if they fall inside a quarter circle. If 785 out of 1000 points land inside, what is the estimate for π?
quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Monte Carlo Methods in Geometry ⭐ What Is Monte Carlo? The other options — '0.785', '2.355', and '1.57' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How can Monte Carlo methods estimate the area of an irregular shape?

options.text: By computing (hits inside / total points) × bounding rectangle area
By dividing the shape into exact triangles
By using the formula length × width

quiz_questions.explanation: The area ≈ (hits inside region / total points) × area of rectangle. Key Concepts — Monte Carlo Methods in Geometry ⭐ What Is Monte Carlo? The other options — 'By measuring its perimeter precisely', 'By dividing the shape into exact triangles', and 'By using the formula length × width' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does accuracy of a Monte Carlo estimate change when the number of samples increases?

options.text: Accuracy doubles with each additional sample

quiz_questions.explanation: Error decreases proportionally to 1/√n, where n is the number of samples. Key Concepts — Monte Carlo Methods in Geometry ⭐ What Is Monte Carlo? The other options — 'Accuracy decreases', 'Accuracy stays the same', and 'Accuracy doubles with each additional sample' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a common application of Monte Carlo methods?
quiz_questions.explanation: Cations are positive ions (formed by losing electrons); anions are negative ions (formed by gaining electrons). Metals typically form cations; nonmetals form anions. The answer is 'Solving linear equations exactly'. The other options — 'Computer graphics rendering', 'Financial risk analysis', and 'Physics particle simulations' — are all valid examples, making 'Solving linear equations exactly' the correct exception.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bounding rectangle has area 200 cm². In a Monte Carlo simulation, 1500 out of 5000 random points land inside an irregular shape. What is the estimated area of the shape?
quiz_questions.explanation: The area of a rectangle is A = length × width. Substitute the given values and calculate. The other options — '75 cm²', '30 cm²', and '100 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a random walk?

options.text: A path that always moves in one direction
A path consisting of successive random steps
A circular path around a fixed point

quiz_questions.explanation: A random walk is a path made of successive random steps. In mathematics and physics, it models diffusion, stock prices, and other stochastic processes. The answer is 'A path consisting of successive random steps'. The other options — 'A path that always moves in one direction', 'A method for sorting data', and 'A circular path around a fixed point' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Inductive Reasoning and Conjecture
summary_sections.content_html:
Inductive Reasoning

Inductive reasoning uses patterns and observations to form a general conclusion called a conjecture. You look at specific cases and make an educated guess about what is always true.

  • Example: 2, 4, 6, 8, ... → Conjecture: the next number is 10.
  • Inductive reasoning does NOT prove something is true — it only suggests it might be.

Conjecture

A conjecture is an unproven statement based on observations. A conjecture remains unproven until it is shown to be either true (via a proof) or false (via a counterexample).

Counterexample

A counterexample is a single example that disproves a conjecture. You only need ONE counterexample to prove a conjecture is false.

  • Conjecture: "All prime numbers are odd." Counterexample: 2 is prime and even.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is inductive reasoning?

options.text: Reasoning based on patterns to form a conjecture
Reasoning that always proves a statement

quiz_questions.explanation: Inductive Reasoning Inductive reasoning uses patterns and observations to form a general conclusion called a conjecture. Inductive reasoning does NOT prove something is true — it only suggests it might be. The other options — 'Reasoning from general to specific', 'Reasoning that always proves a statement', and 'Reasoning based on definitions only' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a conjecture?

options.text: An unproven statement based on observations
A postulate that is assumed true

quiz_questions.explanation: Conjecture A conjecture is an unproven statement based on observations. A conjecture remains unproven until it is shown to be either true (via a proof) or false (via a counterexample). The other options — 'A proven theorem', 'A definition in geometry', and 'A postulate that is assumed true' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many counterexamples are needed to disprove a conjecture?
quiz_questions.explanation: A counterexample is a specific case that disproves a statement. Finding just ONE counterexample proves a universal claim is false. The answer is 'One'. The other options — 'Two', 'Three', and 'It depends on the conjecture' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an example of inductive reasoning?

options.text: Noticing a pattern in several cases and predicting the next one
Using a theorem to prove a statement
Starting with axioms to derive a conclusion
Using logic rules to form a proof

quiz_questions.explanation: Example — 2, 4, 6, 8,.. → Conjecture: the next number is 10. Inductive reasoning does NOT prove something is true — it only suggests it might be. The other options — 'Using a theorem to prove a statement', 'Starting with axioms to derive a conclusion', and 'Using logic rules to form a proof' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: The sequence 1, 4, 9, 16 suggests the next number is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Example — 2, 4, 6, 8,.. → Conjecture: the next number is 10. The other options — '20', '32', and '36' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A counterexample to 'all rectangles are squares' is:

options.text: A rectangle with unequal length and width

quiz_questions.explanation: A counterexample is a specific case that disproves a statement. Finding just ONE counterexample proves a universal claim is false. The answer is 'A rectangle with unequal length and width'. The other options — 'A square with sides of 5', 'A circle', and 'A rhombus' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does inductive reasoning guarantee truth?

options.text: No, it only suggests a pattern
Yes, if there are enough examples

quiz_questions.explanation: Inductive reasoning does NOT prove something is true — it only suggests it might be. Inductive Reasoning Inductive reasoning uses patterns and observations to form a general conclusion called a conjecture. The other options — 'Yes, it proves the statement', 'Yes, if there are enough examples', and 'Only for numerical patterns' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Notes
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Logic ⭐

Statements and Truth Values

A statement (or proposition) is a sentence that is either true (T) or false (F), but not both.

Negation

The negation of a statement p (written ~p or ¬p) has the opposite truth value. If p is true, then ~p is false, and vice versa.

Compound Statements

  • Conjunction (p ∧ q): "p AND q" — true only when BOTH p and q are true.
  • Disjunction (p ∨ q): "p OR q" — true when AT LEAST ONE of p or q is true.

Truth Tables

A truth table shows all possible combinations of truth values for a compound statement. For two statements p and q, there are 4 possible combinations (TT, TF, FT, FF).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a statement in logic?

options.text: A sentence that is either true or false

quiz_questions.explanation: Key Concepts — Logic ⭐ Statements and Truth Values A statement (or proposition) is a sentence that is either true (T) or false (F) , but not both. The other options — 'Any sentence in English', 'A question about math', and 'An unproven conjecture' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the negation of a true statement?
quiz_questions.explanation: Negation (~p or ¬p) reverses the truth value: if p is true, ~p is false, and vice versa. Negation simply adds 'not' to the statement. The answer is 'False'. The other options — 'True', 'Unknown', and 'Undefined' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A conjunction (p ∧ q) is true when:

options.text: Both p and q are true
p is true and q is false

quiz_questions.explanation: Compound Statements Conjunction (p ∧ q) : "p AND q" — true only when BOTH p and q are true. The other options — 'At least one is true', 'Both are false', and 'p is true and q is false' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A disjunction (p ∨ q) is true when:

options.text: At least one of p or q is true
Both p and q are true

quiz_questions.explanation: Disjunction (p ∨ q) : "p OR q" — true when AT LEAST ONE of p or q is true. The other options — 'Both p and q are true', 'Both are false', and 'Neither is true' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many rows does a truth table for two variables have?
quiz_questions.explanation: For two statements p and q, there are 4 possible combinations (TT, TF, FT, FF). Truth Tables A truth table shows all possible combinations of truth values for a compound statement. The other options — '2', '8', and '6' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What symbol represents negation?
quiz_questions.explanation: Negation (~p or ¬p) reverses the truth value: if p is true, ~p is false, and vice versa. Negation simply adds 'not' to the statement. The answer is '~ or ¬'. The other options — '∧', '∨', and '→' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If p is 'It is raining' and p is true, what is ~p?

options.text: 'It is not raining' and it is false
'It is raining' and it is true
'It might rain' and it is unknown
'It is sunny' and it is true

quiz_questions.explanation: Negation (~p or ¬p) reverses the truth value: if p is true, ~p is false, and vice versa. Negation simply adds 'not' to the statement. The answer is ''It is not raining' and it is false'. The other options — ''It is raining' and it is true', ''It might rain' and it is unknown', and ''It is sunny' and it is true' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Conditional Statements
summary_sections.content_html:
Conditional Statement

A conditional statement has the form "If p, then q" (written p → q). The hypothesis is p and the conclusion is q.

  • A conditional is false ONLY when the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false.

Related Conditionals

  • Converse: If q, then p (q → p) — swap hypothesis and conclusion.
  • Inverse: If not p, then not q (~p → ~q) — negate both.
  • Contrapositive: If not q, then not p (~q → ~p) — swap AND negate.

Key Fact

A conditional and its contrapositive always have the same truth value. The converse and inverse also share the same truth value (but may differ from the original).

Biconditional

A biconditional statement (p ↔ q) means "p if and only if q." It is true when both p and q have the same truth value.

quiz_questions.question_text: In 'If p, then q,' what is p called?
quiz_questions.explanation: The hypothesis is p and the conclusion is q. A conditional is false ONLY when the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false. The other options — 'The conclusion', 'The converse', and 'The inverse' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: When is a conditional statement false?

options.text: When the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false

quiz_questions.explanation: A conditional is false ONLY when the hypothesis is true and the conclusion is false. Related Conditionals Converse — If q, then p (q → p) — swap hypothesis and conclusion. The other options — 'When both parts are false', 'When the conclusion is true', and 'When the hypothesis is false' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the contrapositive of 'If p, then q'?

options.text: If not q, then not p
If not p, then not q

quiz_questions.explanation: Contrapositive — If not q, then not p (~q → ~p) — swap AND negate. The other options — 'If q, then p', 'If not p, then not q', and 'If p, then not q' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the converse of 'If it rains, then the ground is wet'?

options.text: If the ground is wet, then it rains
If it does not rain, the ground is not wet
If the ground is not wet, then it does not rain
It rains and the ground is wet

quiz_questions.explanation: The converse of 'If p, then q' is 'If q, then p.' The converse is NOT logically equivalent to the original; it may be true or false independently. The answer is 'If the ground is wet, then it rains'.
